app.name: Cadence
app.description: Multi-tenant AI orchestration platform with hot/warm/cold tier pooling, BYOK, and multi-framework support.

langGraphSupervisor.defaultModelDescription: Model used by all nodes unless overridden per-node

langGraphSupervisor.nodeExecutionTimeoutDescription: Default timeout in seconds for each graph node or agent step (LLM and tool calls; answer nodes use 2×)
langGraphSupervisor.maxMessageContextWindowDescription: Last N messages sent to orchestrator (Min/Max: 5/50)
langGraphSupervisor.autocompactLlmConfigDescription: Config used LLM for message compaction
langGraphSupervisor.autocompactModelDescription: Config used model for message compaction
langGraphSupervisor.suggestionLlmConfigDescription: LLM config used for generating suggestion questions
langGraphSupervisor.suggestionModelDescription: Model used for generating suggestion questions

langGraphSupervisor.modelNodeConfigDescription: Model for this node's LLM config
langGraphSupervisor.modelOverrideDescription: Overrides default model for this node

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.description:
**What it does:**

Central hub for your organization's AI orchestration. Shows:

- **AI App counts** — Total, hot, warm, and cold tier instances
- **Pool statistics** — Shared models, memory usage (sys admin only)
- **Quick actions** — Start chat, view AI Apps, manage AI agents

**Tiers:** Hot = loaded immediately; Warm = pre-warmed; Cold = on-demand.

chat.title: Chat
chat.description:
**What it does:**

Interactive playground to test orchestrators with real conversations.

**How to use:**
1. Select an orchestrator from the sidebar (must be active and ready)
2. Type a message and press Enter
3. View the response, agent steps, and tool sources

**Features:**
- Streaming responses with progress
- Expandable steps and sources per message
- Session persists until you clear

profile.title: Profile
profile.description:
**What it does:**

Your personal account settings.

**Sections:**
- **Account** — User ID, role (read-only)
- **Profile** — Display name, email (editable)
- **Password** — Change password (requires current password)

Changes apply immediately. Your role determines access across organizations.

aiApps.title: Orchestrators
aiApps.description:
**What it does:**

Create and manage AI App instances that run your AI agents (plugin packages).

**Actions:**
- **Load** — Start the AI App (uses pool memory)
- **Unload** — Stop and free memory
- **Activate** — Enable for use; **Deactivate** — Disable
- **Clone** — Copy config to create a new instance

**Columns:** Name, framework, mode, tier, status, ready indicator.

agentStore.title: Agents Store
agentStore.description:
**What it does:**

Browse and manage AI agents (plugin ZIPs) that extend AI App capabilities.

**Types:**
- **Official** — Platform-wide, available to all orgs
- **Org** — Per-organization, uploaded by org admins

**Actions:** Enable/disable, view details, configure settings. Each package maps to the plugin API (`/api/.../plugins`) and supplies tools AI Apps use at runtime.

orgIdentity.title: Organization
orgIdentity.description:
**What it shows:**

Read-only identity of your current organization:

- **Organization ID** — Unique identifier
- **Slug** — URL-friendly name
- **Domain** — Assigned domain (e.g. acme.com)
- **Subscription tier** — free, pro, enterprise
- **Status** — active, suspended, etc.

**Note:** Only system admins can change these values.

orgProfile.title: Organization Profile
orgProfile.description:
**What it does:**

Editable public profile visible to org members.

**Fields:**
- Display name, contact email, website
- Logo URL, country, timezone
- Description (brief org summary)

**Who can edit:** Org admins only. Used for branding and contact info.

members.title: Members
members.description:
**What it does:**

Manage who belongs to your organization.

**Actions:**
- **Add member** — By email, username, or user ID
- **Promote** — Grant org admin rights
- **Demote** — Remove org admin rights
- **Remove** — Remove from organization

**Who can manage:** Org admins only. Members can belong to multiple orgs.

llmConfigs.title: LLM Configurations
llmConfigs.description:
**What it does:**

Bring Your Own Key (BYOK) — store API keys for LLM providers.

**Supported:** OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Groq, Azure, LiteLLM, TensorZero, Bifrost

**Per config:** Name, provider, API key, optional base URL, API version (Azure)

**Usage:** Orchestrators select a config when running. Disabled configs are hidden. Configs in use cannot be deleted until unlinked.

centralPoints.title: Central Points
centralPoints.description:
**What it does:**

Stable API endpoints that abstract orchestrator identity.

**Why use them:**
- Clients use a **central point ID** — never the orchestrator ID
- Swap orchestrators behind the endpoint without client changes
- Private (auth required) or public (no auth)

**Flow:** Create central point → assign orchestrator → clients call `/api/chat/completion` with `X-CENTRAL-ID` header.

aiApps.title: Orchestrator Settings
aiApps.description:
**What it does:**

Default values pre-filled when creating **new** orchestrators.

**Defaults:**
- Default LLM config
- Default model name
- Default max tokens
- Node execution timeout (seconds)

**Important:** Existing orchestrators are **not** affected. Only new instances get these values.

orgs.title: Organizations
orgs.description:
**What it does:**

System-wide organization management (sys admin only).

**Actions:**
- Create organizations (name, domain, tier)
- View and edit org details
- Set tier quotas and rate limits

**Tier quotas** control max orchestrators, members, LLM configs, messages, etc. per organization.

orgDetail.title: Organization Details
orgDetail.description:
**What it does:**

Edit a single organization (sys admin only).

**Sections:**
- **Profile** — Display name, domain, tier, contact info
- **Tier quotas** — Max orchestrators, members, central points, messages, LLM configs, rate limits
- **Members** — Add, promote, demote, remove

**Preview** shows current usage vs. quota limits.

users.title: Users
users.description:
**What it does:**

Platform user management (sys admin only).

**Actions:**
- Create users (username, email, optional password)
- Edit username, email, system admin flag
- Delete users (removes all org memberships)

**System admin** — Full platform access. Users can belong to multiple organizations with different roles.

systemPlugins.title: Agent store
systemPlugins.description:
**What it does:**

Manage AI agents (plugin ZIPs) available to **all** organizations (sys admin only).

**Tabs:** Official | Org (per-organization)

**Actions:**
- Upload AI agent (ZIP)
- Enable/disable versions
- View details and config

Org admins enable official agents for their org. Org-scoped agents are uploaded per org in **Agent store**.

globalSettings.title: Global Settings
globalSettings.description:
**What it does:**

Platform-wide defaults (sys admin only).

**Sections:**
- **Subscription tiers** — Quota limits per tier (orchestrators, members, messages, etc.)
- **LLM providers** — Model catalog per provider
- **Feature settings** — Pool, caching, rate limiting, etc.

**Override:** Some settings can be overridden per-organization. Tier quotas are global.

pool.title: Instance Pool
pool.description:
**What it does:**

Real-time pool statistics (sys admin only).

**Metrics:**
- Total instances, hot/warm/cold tier counts
- Shared models and bundles
- Memory estimate

**Auto-refresh:** Every 30 seconds. Use Refresh to update manually.

health.title: System Health
health.description:
**What it does:**

System and service status (sys admin only).

**Checks:**
- **PostgreSQL, Redis, MongoDB** — Database connectivity
- **Orchestrator instances** — Loaded instances, ready state

**Use for:** Diagnosing outages, verifying instance health before traffic.

displayName.title: Display Name
displayName.description:
**What it is:**

Public name shown in the UI and integrations.

**Use for:** Branding, user identification, contact display. Not used for authentication.

provider.title: Provider
provider.description:
**What it is:**

LLM provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.).

**Effect:** Determines API format and endpoint. Each provider has different models and pricing. Select the provider that holds your API key.

model.title: Model
model.description:
**What it is:**

Model identifier (e.g. `gpt-4o`, `gemini-2.0-flash`).

**Rule:** Must be supported by the selected provider. Check provider docs for valid model IDs. Use catalog for platform-managed models.

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description:
**What it is:**

Your API key for the provider.

**Security:** Stored encrypted. Never shown in logs or UI after save. Rotate keys if compromised.

endpoint.title: Endpoint URL
endpoint.description:
**What it is:**

Stable URL for chat completion: `POST /api/chat/completion`

**Headers required:**
- `X-ORG-ID` — Organization ID
- `X-CENTRAL-ID` — Central point ID
- `Authorization: Bearer` — For private (optional for public)

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description:
**Private central points:**

Clients must send `Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN` in every request.

**Public:** No auth header required. Use for open demos or internal tools.

framework.title: Framework
framework.description:
**What it is:**

Orchestration framework:

- **LangGraph** — Multi-node graphs, state machines
- **Google ADK** — Agent Development Kit
- **OpenAI Agents** — OpenAI agent framework

**Effect:** Determines graph structure, node types, and execution model. Immutable after creation.

llmConfig.title: LLM Configuration
llmConfig.description:
**What it is:**

LLM config used by this orchestrator for inference.

**Source:** Must be from your org's LLM Configurations (Settings → LLM Configs).

**Supervisor mode:** Can override per-node. Non-supervisor uses this config globally.

createBasic.title: Basic
createBasic.description:
**What it does:**

Core identity and runtime settings for the new orchestrator.

**Fields:**
- **Name** — Unique identifier (10–55 chars). Used in URLs and API.
- **Framework** — LangGraph, Google ADK, or OpenAI Agents. **Immutable after creation.**
- **Mode** — Supervisor (multi-agent) or other modes. Depends on framework.
- **Initial tier** — Hot (load immediately), Warm (pre-warm), or Cold (on-demand). Affects pool memory.

createPlugins.title: AI agents
createPlugins.description:
**What it does:**

Select AI agents (plugin packages) that add capabilities to this AI App.

**Flow:**
1. Click **Add AI agent** to open the selector
2. Search and add one or more agents (required)
3. Configure each agent's settings below the table

**Note:** Each agent can have required fields. Validation runs before create. You can remove agents before saving.

supervisorLlmConfig.title: Supervisor Node Settings
supervisorLlmConfig.description:
**What it does:**

Override LLM settings per node in the supervisor graph.

**Nodes:**
- **Router** — Classifies incoming messages
- **Planner** — Plans steps
- **Synthesizer** — Final response
- **Clarifier, Responder, Validation, Error Handler** — Specialized nodes

**Per node:** Override LLM config, model, temperature, max tokens, timeout. Leave empty to use the default from the LLM Config card.

nodeConfig.title: Node Config
nodeConfig.description:
**What it does:**

Override LLM settings per node in the supervisor graph.

**Nodes:**
- **Router** — Classifies incoming messages
- **Planner** — Plans steps
- **Synthesizer** — Final response
- **Clarifier, Responder, Validation, Error Handler** — Specialized nodes

**Per node:** Override LLM config, model, temperature, max tokens, timeout. Leave empty to use the default from LLM Config above.

editBasic.title: Basic
editBasic.description:
**What it does:**

Edit metadata and tier. Framework and mode are **immutable** after creation.

**Editable:**
- **Name** — Display name (10–200 chars)
- **Tier** — Hot, Warm, or Cold

**Supervisor only:** LLM config is in the Supervisor Settings section below.

**Non-supervisor:** Select LLM config here — it's used for all inference.

additionalSettings.title: Additional Settings
additionalSettings.description:
**What it does:**

Controls LLM call behavior and monitoring for this orchestrator.

**Fields:**
- **Max Message Context Window** — Max messages kept in context per turn (Min/Max: 5/50).
- **Max Token Context Window** — Token budget for context (Min/Max: 4096/32000).
- **LLM Call Timeout** — Timeout in seconds per LLM/tool call (answer nodes use 2×).
- **Auto-compact Messages** — Automatically summarize old messages when context is full.
- **Monitoring** — Langfuse integration for tracing LLM calls.

llmConfig.title: LLM Config
llmConfig.description:
**What it does:**

Sets the default LLM used by all nodes in this orchestrator.

**Fields:**
- **Default LLM config** — Required. Select the provider + credential config from your org.
- **Default model** — Model used by all nodes unless overridden per-node.
- **Temperature** — Controls output randomness (0.0–2.0).
- **Max tokens** — Max output tokens per response (Min/Max: 1k/4k).

**Note:** These defaults can be overridden per-node in the Supervisor Node Settings card.

centralPoints.selectOrgDescription: Choose an organization from the menu above to manage central points.
centralPoints.title: Central Points
centralPoints.description: Stable API endpoints for orchestrators. Clients use central point ID; you can swap orchestrators without client changes.
centralPoints.noCenterPoints: No central points yet. Create one to get started.
centralPoints.name: Name
centralPoints.editSubtitle: Edit settings and view API integration guides.
centralPoints.apiIntegrationDescription: Use the central point ID below to send chat requests. The endpoint stays stable — swap orchestrators any time without updating your clients.
centralPoints.privateAuthDescription: This central point requires authentication. Include an Authorization: Bearer token header in all requests.
centralPoints.publicNoAuthDescription: This central point is publicly accessible. No authentication header is required.

overview.title: Overview
overview.subtitle: A quick introduction to Cadence.
overview.intro: Cadence helps teams deploy and manage AI assistants. Create assistants, add your AI provider, and chat — no coding required.
overview.feature1: Multi-tenant — each team has its own workspace with isolated data and settings
overview.feature2: Bring your own AI keys — use OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, and others; your keys, your control
overview.feature3: No-code setup — configure assistants through the UI, no programming needed
overview.feature4: Real-time chat — see responses stream in with full visibility into how the assistant works
overview.feature5: Extensible — add custom tools via plugins (web search, calendar, APIs, and more)
overview.feature6: Stable endpoints — connect apps and integrations via Central Points without hardcoding IDs
overview.feature7: Role-based access — control who can manage assistants, settings, or just chat

sections.orchestrator: Your AI assistant — create, configure, and manage.
sections.chat: Talk to your assistants in real time.
sections.plugins: Add tools like web search or calendar to your assistants.
sections.llmConfigs: Your AI provider keys — OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, and more.
sections.centralPoints: Stable endpoints for apps and integrations.
sections.orgs: Teams and workspaces with isolated data.
sections.roles: Who can do what — admin, member, and more.
sections.frameworks: Technical execution engines — LangGraph, Google ADK, OpenAI.

orchestrator.title: Orchestrator
orchestrator.subtitle: Your AI assistant — configured once, ready to chat.
orchestrator.intro: An orchestrator is your AI assistant. You choose its abilities (plugins), connect your AI provider, and start chatting. Each assistant runs independently and can be loaded or unloaded to manage resources.
orchestrator.bullet1: Create assistants from the Orchestrators page
orchestrator.bullet2: Choose Hot (fastest), Warm (quick), or Cold (lower cost, slower startup) based on your needs
orchestrator.bullet3: Load and unload assistants to manage memory across many instances
orchestrator.bullet4: Clone an existing assistant to reuse its configuration
orchestrator.bullet5: Activate or deactivate without losing your setup

faq1.question: What is an orchestrator?
faq1.answer: An orchestrator is a running AI assistant. It combines an execution engine, a mode (how it reasons), and a set of tools (plugins). You configure it once, then chat with it or connect it to your apps.

faq2.question: What do Hot, Warm, and Cold mean?
faq2.answer: Hot = loaded in memory and ready instantly. Warm = pre-warmed for fast startup. Cold = loads on first use (slower start, lower cost). You can change tier at any time.

faq3.question: Can I run multiple assistants at once?
faq3.answer: Yes. You can have many orchestrators. The platform manages memory across all of them. Your organization's subscription tier determines how many can be loaded at the same time.

faq4.question: What is a plugin?
faq4.answer: A plugin is a packaged capability — a set of tools your assistant can call. For example, a web search plugin lets your assistant search the web; a calendar plugin lets it read events. You choose which plugins to attach when creating an orchestrator.

faq5.question: Can I change plugins after creating an orchestrator?
faq5.answer: Plugin settings and versions can be updated at any time from the orchestrator's detail page. However, the framework and mode are fixed after creation — to change those, clone the orchestrator and start fresh.

faq6.question: What happens when I change a setting?
faq6.answer: Hot-tier orchestrators reload automatically to pick up the new setting. Cold and Warm orchestrators apply the change on their next load. You will see a brief interruption for hot-tier assistants during the reload.

faq7.question: Which framework should I choose?
faq7.answer: LangGraph is best for complex multi-step workflows that need fine-grained control. Google ADK is the natural fit if you are on Google Cloud or using Gemini models. OpenAI Agents is the quickest way to get started if you already use the OpenAI API.

crudSection.title: Managing Orchestrators
crudSection.intro: Everything you need to create, configure, and manage your assistants is on the Orchestrators page.
crudSection.create: Create — give your assistant a name, choose a framework and mode, pick a tier, and attach plugins. The framework and mode cannot be changed after creation.
crudSection.view: View — see status, current tier, load state, and linked plugins at a glance from the orchestrator list or detail page.
crudSection.edit: Edit — update the display name, switch tiers, change plugin settings, or swap to a newer plugin version at any time.
crudSection.clone: Clone — duplicate an existing orchestrator's full configuration as a starting point for a new one.
crudSection.delete: Delete — permanently removes the orchestrator and all its history. This cannot be undone.
crudSection.activate: Activate / Deactivate — pause an orchestrator without deleting it. Deactivated orchestrators do not accept chat requests.

tiersSection.title: Loading & Performance Tiers
tiersSection.intro: Each orchestrator runs in one of three tiers that control how it is kept in memory and how fast it responds.

hot.name: Hot
hot.desc: Loaded in memory and ready instantly. Best for frequently used assistants where response speed matters.

warm.name: Warm
warm.desc: Pre-warmed for fast startup. Slightly slower than Hot on first response but uses fewer resources when idle.

cold.name: Cold
cold.desc: Loads on first use. Slowest to start but uses the fewest resources — ideal for occasional-use assistants.

tiersSection.note: You can change an orchestrator's tier at any time. You can also manually load or unload a Hot-tier assistant from its detail page without changing the tier.

featuresSection.title: What You Can Do
featuresSection.intro: Once an orchestrator is loaded, here is what you can do with it:
featuresSection.bullet1: Chat in real time — send messages and see responses stream back with full reasoning steps visible
featuresSection.bullet2: Inspect steps — expand each response to see which tools were called and what the assistant was thinking
featuresSection.bullet3: Connect to a Central Point — assign a stable named endpoint so clients can reach the assistant without knowing its ID
featuresSection.bullet4: Clone and experiment — duplicate a working assistant to try new plugins or settings safely
featuresSection.bullet5: Share via your org — all org members can chat with any active orchestrator in the organization

pluginLoadSection.title: How Plugin Loading Works
pluginLoadSection.intro: When an orchestrator starts up, each of its plugins goes through a nine-step pipeline before any tool becomes available to the assistant.

step1.title: Pool Check
step1.desc: When a chat request arrives, the pool checks if the orchestrator is already in memory. If not, it acquires a lock and loads from the database.

step2.title: Instance Config Retrieved
step2.desc: The DB record for the orchestrator is fetched. It contains an active_plugins list (e.g. system:com.acme.search{'@'}1.0) and a plugin_settings map with per-plugin overrides.

step3.title: Plugin Manager Created
step3.desc: The factory constructs an SDKPluginManager, handing it the filesystem root, system plugins directory, optional S3 plugin store, and bundle cache.

step4.title: Contract Resolution
step4.desc: For each plugin spec, the manager checks the in-memory registry first. On a miss it looks up the versioned directory on disk. If S3 is configured, it downloads and extracts the plugin zip before proceeding.

step5.title: Module Loading
step5.desc: The plugin's plugin.py is scanned with Python's AST parser to reject any dangerous imports (os, subprocess, socket, etc.). If it passes, the file is loaded dynamically via importlib and the BasePlugin subclass is extracted and registered.

step6.title: Validation
step6.desc: The SDK validates that the plugin class has the required interface (create_agent(), get_metadata()). If the class defines validate_dependencies(), that runs too. Failures raise PluginValidationError and abort loading.

step7.title: Bundle Assembly
step7.desc: The plugin agent is instantiated. Settings are resolved in three layers: schema defaults → instance overrides from DB → validated final values. Tools are extracted, converted to the framework's native format, and bound to a dedicated LLM model. The result is an SDKPluginBundle.

step8.title: Tool Aggregation
step8.desc: Once all bundles are built, a tool collector flattens every plugin's tools into a single list with reverse mappings (tool name → plugin id) and per-tool metadata flags.

step9.title: Graph Compiled
step9.desc: The framework-specific orchestrator (e.g. LangGraphSupervisor) receives the full tool list, binds it to the planner model, constructs the stateful execution graph, and compiles it. The orchestrator is now ready to handle requests.

pluginLoadSection.securityNote: Security: every plugin file is parsed with Python's AST before execution. Any file that imports blocked modules (os, subprocess, socket, and others) is rejected before the code ever runs.
pluginLoadSection.cacheNote: Performance: stateless plugin bundles are cached by pid + version + settings hash. A second orchestrator using the same plugin version reuses the cached bundle instead of re-loading the module.

frameworks.title: Frameworks
frameworks.subtitle: LangGraph, Google ADK, and OpenAI Agents SDK — each with different provider support and execution models.
frameworks.intro: The framework determines how your orchestrator executes internally — graph topology, agent handoff patterns, state management, and tool-calling conventions. Each framework integrates with different AI providers and has different strengths for different use cases.
frameworks.bullet1: LangGraph: multi-node state machines with full node-level override support
frameworks.bullet2: Google ADK: integrates with Gemini models and Google tooling
frameworks.bullet3: OpenAI Agents SDK: structured agent handoffs using OpenAI conventions
frameworks.bullet4: Framework is selected at creation and is immutable
frameworks.bullet5: Provider compatibility varies by framework — check docs before choosing

faq1.question: What is LangGraph?
faq1.answer: LangGraph is a graph-based orchestration framework where nodes represent AI agents or processing steps and edges define control flow. It supports state machines and multi-agent coordination.

faq2.question: What is Google ADK?
faq2.answer: Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) is Google's framework for building and deploying AI agents. It integrates with Google's Gemini models and tooling ecosystem.

faq3.question: What is OpenAI Agents SDK?
faq3.answer: The OpenAI Agents SDK is OpenAI's framework for building multi-agent systems with handoffs, tool use, and structured orchestration patterns.

executionSection.title: How Each Framework Executes
executionSection.intro: Cadence maps each framework to a set of specialized classes. The factory looks up the (framework, mode) pair in a registry and assembles the adapter, plugin manager, and orchestrator before returning a ready instance.

langgraph.model: Compiles a stateful StateGraph with eight specialized nodes: Router, Planner, Executor, Synthesizer, Clarifier, Responder, Validator, and Error Handler. Control-flow edges determine which node runs next based on structured output from the planner.
langgraph.tools: Plugin tools are converted to LangChain StructuredTool instances and bound to the planner via model.bind_tools(). A ToolNode handles actual execution and wraps results in ToolMessage objects that feed back into the graph.

adk.model: Creates a separate LlmAgent for each pipeline stage. The planner agent receives all tools directly and handles its own function-call loop — no separate executor node. A custom BaseAgent subclass wraps the full pipeline and drives the ADK runner.
adk.tools: Plugin tools are converted to ADK FunctionTool instances and passed directly to LlmAgent(tools=all_tools). ADK's native loop processes function calls and yields structured event objects that the pipeline collects.

openai.model: Not yet implemented. The class exists as a registered placeholder that raises UnsupportedOperationError on use. Implementation is planned for a future release.

compatSection.title: Provider Compatibility
compatSection.intro: Each framework works with a specific set of LLM providers. The provider is configured in your LLM Config and must be compatible with the framework you select.

factory.title: Backend Registration Signature
factory.frameworkType: String identifier for the framework, e.g. langgraph or google_adk.
factory.mode: Orchestration mode the registration applies to, e.g. supervisor.
factory.adapterClass: Class that adapts the framework-native runner to Cadence's plugin and settings model.
factory.orchestratorClass: Top-level class that builds the graph/agent pipeline and exposes the run interface.
factory.streamingWrapperClass: Wraps the framework's native streaming output into Cadence SSE events.
factory.registryNote: The factory looks up (framework_type, mode) at orchestrator creation time. Unregistered pairs raise an UnsupportedBackendError.

streaming.title: Framework Streaming Internals
streaming.langraph: LangGraph: streaming via .astream_events() on the compiled graph. Each emitted event is mapped to an SSE event type.
streaming.adk: Google ADK: no native parallel tool calls. Events are yielded from the ADK runner and mapped individually to SSE events.

list.desc: Returns the list of registered (framework, mode) pairs and their implementation status.

modes.title: Orchestration Modes
modes.subtitle: Supervisor and Grounded modes are fully implemented. Additional modes are in preview.
modes.intro: Modes define the high-level agent topology — how many agents run, how they hand off work, and how the final response is assembled. Supervisor mode uses a planner-synthesizer architecture where a router classifies incoming messages, the planner decomposes tasks, tool-calling agents handle subtasks, and the synthesizer assembles the final answer.
modes.bullet1: Supervisor: multi-node graph with Router, Planner, Synthesizer, and tool agents
modes.bullet2: Per-node AI config, model, temperature, and timeout overrides in Supervisor mode
modes.bullet3: Mode is set at creation and cannot be changed later
modes.bullet4: Grounded: single scoped agent anchored to a resource with LLM-based scope validation

grounded.desc: Single scoped agent anchored to a resource. The agent operates within a defined scope boundary — an LLM validation pass checks that each response stays on-topic before it is returned to the user. Uses BaseScopedAgent which requires load_anchor() and build_scope_rules() implementations.

faq1.question: What is Supervisor mode?
faq1.answer: Supervisor mode uses a multi-node architecture with specialized nodes: Router, Planner, Synthesizer, Clarifier, Responder, Validation, and Error Handler. The router classifies each message, the planner breaks it into steps, and the synthesizer composes the final answer.

faq2.question: Are other modes available?
faq2.answer: Grounded mode is fully implemented alongside Supervisor. Additional modes (Coordinator, Handoff, Pipeline, Reflection, Ensemble, Hierarchical) are in preview and planned for future releases.

faq3.question: Can I change the mode after creation?
faq3.answer: No. The framework and mode are immutable after an orchestrator is created. To change mode, clone the orchestrator and select a different mode.

supervisorConfig.title: SupervisorMode Config Fields
supervisorConfig.maxAgentHops: Maximum number of planner→executor cycles before the supervisor forces a final answer.
supervisorConfig.parallelToolCalls: Allow the planner to call multiple tools in a single LLM round-trip.
supervisorConfig.llmValidation: Run an extra LLM pass to validate the synthesizer output before returning it.
supervisorConfig.autoCompact: Automatically compact the graph state when it exceeds the model context window.
supervisorConfig.nodeTimeout: Per-node execution wall-clock timeout in seconds. Applies to all nodes unless overridden.
supervisorConfig.nodeOverridesNote: Per-node overrides are supported for all seven Supervisor nodes:
supervisorConfig.nodeOverridesFields: Each node accepts: llm_config_id, model_name, prompt, temperature, max_tokens, timeout.

groundedConfig.title: GroundedMode Config Fields
groundedConfig.scopeRules: Natural-language rules the validator uses to check that responses stay on-topic.
groundedConfig.maxToolRounds: Maximum number of tool-call rounds before the agent must produce a final answer.
groundedConfig.enabledValidator: Run the LLM scope-validator before returning each response.
groundedConfig.nodeTimeout: Wall-clock timeout in seconds for the single Processor node.
groundedConfig.stateNote: Grounded mode tracks per-session state: thread_id, resource_id, anchor_context, context_loaded, context_was_fresh.

plugins.title: Plugins
plugins.subtitle: Upload ZIP packages to extend orchestrator capabilities with custom tools.
plugins.intro: Plugins are Python packages that expose tools to orchestrators. They follow the Cadence SDK conventions — annotated function definitions that the orchestrator can invoke during a conversation. Store (system plugins) are available platform-wide; org plugins are scoped to the uploading organization.
plugins.bullet1: Upload plugins as ZIP packages via Plugins → Upload
plugins.bullet2: Store (system plugins) are managed by system admins and available to all orgs
plugins.bullet3: Org plugins are uploaded per-organization and private to that org
plugins.bullet4: Plugins can declare required configuration settings filled per-orchestrator
plugins.bullet5: Enable or disable specific plugin versions independently

faq1.question: What format are plugins in?
faq1.answer: Plugins are ZIP packages containing a Python module or package. They expose tools via the Cadence SDK using standard function definitions with type annotations.

faq2.question: What is the difference between Store (system plugins) and org plugins?
faq2.answer: Store (system plugins) are uploaded by system admins and available to all organizations. Org plugins are uploaded by org admins and available only within that organization.

faq3.question: Can a plugin require configuration?
faq3.answer: Yes. Plugins can declare configuration settings (e.g. API keys, thresholds) that must be filled when adding the plugin to an orchestrator. Required settings block orchestrator creation if not filled.

anatomySection.title: Plugin Anatomy
anatomySection.intro: A plugin is built from four base classes packaged in a single plugin.py file. The platform discovers the BasePlugin subclass automatically — no registration file needed.

basePlugin.desc: Stateless factory class. Provides metadata and creates agent instances on demand. All methods are static — BasePlugin is never instantiated itself.
basePlugin.method1: get_metadata() → PluginMetadata  [required] declare identity, version, and capabilities
basePlugin.method2: create_agent() → BaseAgent  [required] return a fresh agent instance on every load
basePlugin.method3: validate_dependencies() → List[str]  [optional] return error strings or empty list if OK

baseAgent.desc: Abstract base class. Holds runtime state and exposes tools to the framework. Extend via BaseSpecializedAgent (for multi-agent modes) or BaseScopedAgent (for grounded mode). initialize() is called with the resolved settings dict before any tool runs.
baseAgent.method1: get_tools() → List[UvTool]  [required] return all tool instances this agent provides
baseAgent.method2: initialize(config: Dict[str, Any])  [optional] receives resolved settings on load
baseAgent.method3: async cleanup()  [optional] release resources when the orchestrator unloads

specializedAgent.desc: Extends BaseAgent for use in multi-agent orchestration modes (e.g. Supervisor). Adds a system prompt that the framework injects as context when the agent is invoked by the planner.
specializedAgent.method1: get_system_prompt() → str  [required] return the agent system instruction passed to the LLM

scopedAgent.desc: Extends BaseAgent for use in Grounded mode. The agent is anchored to a specific resource and operates within a scope boundary validated by an LLM pass before each response is returned.
scopedAgent.method1: load_anchor() → Any  [required] load and return the resource this agent is anchored to
scopedAgent.method2: build_scope_rules() → str  [required] return a string describing what is in and out of scope

metadata.desc: Immutable descriptor returned by get_metadata(). The pid field is the plugin's permanent identity across version upgrades — changing it creates a new plugin.
metadata.field1: pid — reverse-domain unique ID, e.g. com.acme.my_plugin (immutable after registration)
metadata.field2: name, version, description — display fields; version must follow semver (e.g. 1.2.0)
metadata.field3: capabilities — list of capability tag strings shown in the plugin catalogue
metadata.field4: dependencies — pip requirement strings (e.g. requests>=2.28) installed at load time
metadata.field5: stateless — set to False if the agent holds cross-request state; affects caching behaviour

toolsSection.title: Defining Tools
toolsSection.intro: Each tool is a UvTool instance, most easily created with the {'@'}uvtool decorator on an async function. The decorator infers name and description from the function name and docstring if not specified.

field1.desc: Passed verbatim to the LLM planner. Clear, specific descriptions are the single most important factor in whether the planner calls the tool correctly and with the right arguments.

field2.desc: Pydantic BaseModel subclass that validates the planner's arguments before the function runs. Field descriptions are appended to the tool prompt the planner sees.

field3.desc: When True, the raw tool result is forwarded to the client immediately, before the synthesizer produces the final response. Useful for search results and data tables.

field4.desc: Callable applied to the raw result before streaming. Use it to strip internal fields, secrets, or large blobs from what the client sees while the full result still flows through the pipeline.

field5.desc: Instructs the orchestrator to run an LLM validation pass on the tool output before handing it to the synthesizer. Adds latency but catches tool errors before they reach the user.

settingsSection.title: Declaring Plugin Settings
settingsSection.intro: Apply the {'@'}plugin_settings decorator to the BasePlugin class to declare configuration that orchestrator owners must fill. Values are resolved through the three-tier cascade and passed to agent.initialize().

field1.desc: Machine-readable identifier. Becomes the key in the config dict passed to agent.initialize(config).

field2.desc: Value type — one of: str, int, float, bool, list, dict. The platform type-checks user input against this.

field3.desc: If true, the orchestrator cannot be saved until this setting is provided. Blocks creation, not just a warning.

field4.desc: Marks the value as secret — encrypted at rest, masked in the UI, and redacted from logs. Use for API keys and passwords.

field5.desc: Fallback used when the orchestrator owner leaves the field blank. Must match the declared type.

uploadSection.title: Upload & Validation Pipeline
uploadSection.intro: Every uploaded plugin passes through an eight-step pipeline. A failure at any step rejects the upload and returns the error to the uploader.

step1.title: Package
step1.desc: Put plugin.py and any supporting modules in a directory. Use build_plugin_zip() from the SDK or zip the directory manually. __pycache__ and *.pyc are excluded automatically.

step2.title: Upload
step2.desc: Go to Plugins → Upload. System admins see a scope selector (system or org). Select the ZIP file and submit. File size is capped at the configured limit.

step3.title: Extraction
step3.desc: The server extracts the ZIP into a versioned directory under the configured plugin root. The directory is named using the pid and version from the metadata.

step4.title: Class Discovery
step4.desc: plugin.py is scanned for a class that inherits from BasePlugin. The upload is rejected if none is found or if more than one BasePlugin subclass is defined in the file.

step5.title: AST Security Scan
step5.desc: plugin.py is parsed with Python's AST before any code runs. Any import of a blocked module (os, subprocess, socket, sys, shutil, and others) immediately rejects the upload.

step6.title: Metadata Validation
step6.desc: get_metadata() is called. The platform checks that pid follows reverse-domain format, version follows semver, and all required fields are present and correctly typed.

step7.title: Dependency Check
step7.desc: If the plugin defines validate_dependencies(), it is called. Any error strings returned are displayed to the uploader and abort registration. An empty list means all clear.

step8.title: Registration
step8.desc: The plugin record and version entry are written to the database. The plugin is now available in the catalogue and can be added to orchestrators.

chat.title: Chat
chat.subtitle: Real-time streaming chat with AI agents via orchestrators or Central Points.
chat.intro: The Chat page is an interactive playground for testing and using orchestrators. Messages stream in real time. Each response shows the agent's internal steps — which nodes fired, which tools were called, and what intermediate outputs were produced — giving you full visibility into agent reasoning.
chat.bullet1: Select a loaded orchestrator from the sidebar to start chatting
chat.bullet2: Responses stream token-by-token in real time
chat.bullet3: Expand "steps" and "sources" per message to inspect agent reasoning
chat.bullet4: Session persists until cleared — useful for multi-turn conversations
chat.bullet5: Also accessible via Central Points for stable endpoint routing

faq1.question: How does streaming work?
faq1.answer: Chat uses Server-Sent Events to stream tokens from the orchestrator in real time. You see each token as it is generated rather than waiting for the full response.

faq2.question: What are "steps" in a chat response?
faq2.answer: Steps show the internal reasoning of the orchestrator — which nodes fired, which tools were called, and what intermediate outputs were produced. Expand them to debug or understand agent behavior.

faq3.question: What is a Central Point in chat context?
faq3.answer: A Central Point is a stable named endpoint you can assign to an orchestrator. Clients send messages to the central point ID; you can swap orchestrators behind it without changing client code.

techSection.title: Streaming & API Reference
techSection.intro: Chat uses Server-Sent Events (SSE). Each event follows the wire format below. The connection stays open until the assistant finishes or an error occurs.

agent.desc: Node execution progress update — includes a display key (i18n), progress percentage, and fallback label.

message.desc: Token chunk (or full content) from a token-streaming node. role is always assistant.

tool.desc: Raw tool output streamed directly before the synthesizer runs. Includes the calling plugin ID.

suggestion.desc: Follow-up question chunk generated after the main response completes.

metadata.desc: Session and routing metadata. Sent once at the start of the stream.

direct.desc: Direct orchestrator chat. Requires org_admin or member role in that org.

centralPoint.desc: Chat via a Central Point. No auth required if the central point is public; Bearer token required if private.

techSection.streamingNodes: Token-streaming nodes in Supervisor mode: Synthesizer, Clarifier, Responder, ErrorHandler. In Grounded mode: Processor.

llmConfigs.title: LLM Configs (BYOK)
llmConfigs.subtitle: Add your own API keys for OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Azure, Groq, and more.
llmConfigs.intro: Cadence is Bring Your Own Key (BYOK). You store your provider API keys in LLM Configs; your orchestrators reference those configs by name. Keys are stored encrypted, never logged, and never returned to the UI after saving. Each organization manages its own set of configs independently.
llmConfigs.bullet1: Supported: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Groq, Azure OpenAI, LiteLLM, TensorZero, Bifrost
llmConfigs.bullet2: Each config has a name, provider, API key, and optional base URL
llmConfigs.bullet3: Azure requires an API version in addition to the key
llmConfigs.bullet4: Disable a config to hide it without deleting — active orchestrators keep working
llmConfigs.bullet5: Configs in use by active orchestrators cannot be deleted until unlinked

faq1.question: What providers are supported?
faq1.answer: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google (Gemini), Groq, Azure OpenAI, LiteLLM, TensorZero, and Bifrost. Each requires an API key and optionally a base URL.

faq2.question: Are API keys stored securely?
faq2.answer: Yes. API keys are stored encrypted and never returned or logged after saving. Rotate keys in Settings → LLM Configs if compromised.

faq3.question: Can different orchestrators use different LLM configs?
faq3.answer: Yes. Each orchestrator selects its own LLM config. In Supervisor mode you can also override the config and model per-node for fine-grained control.

techSection.title: Provider & API Reference

openai.note: Standard OpenAI-compatible API key. No additional config required.

anthropic.note: Anthropic API key. Supports claude-* model families.

google.note: Google AI Studio key. Supports gemini-* models.

groq.note: Groq API key. Fast inference via groq.com.

litellm.note: Proxy any provider via LiteLLM. Requires additional_config: model_prefix or custom base_url.

tensorzero.note: TensorZero gateway. Configure base_url to point to your gateway.

bifrost.note: Bifrost proxy. Configure base_url to point to your Bifrost instance.

isEnabled.desc: When false, the config is hidden from new orchestrators but existing runtime references remain active.

additionalConfig.desc: Provider-specific extra settings. Azure requires deployment_id and api_version. LiteLLM requires model_prefix.

baseUrl.desc: Override the provider's default API base URL. Required for self-hosted gateways (LiteLLM, Bifrost, TensorZero).

techSection.lifecycleNote: Soft-delete is blocked while any active orchestrator holds a runtime reference to the config.

list.desc: List all LLM configs for the org.

create.desc: Create a new LLM config. Provider and API key are required.

update.desc: Patch one or more fields on an existing config.

delete.desc: Soft-delete a config. Blocked if an active orchestrator references it.

models.desc: Fetch the model catalog for a provider. Used to populate the model picker in the UI.

centralPoints.title: Central Points
centralPoints.subtitle: Stable named endpoints that abstract away orchestrator instance IDs; public or private visibility.
centralPoints.intro: A Central Point is an indirection layer between your clients and an orchestrator. Instead of calling a specific orchestrator ID, clients call a stable Central Point ID. You can swap the backing orchestrator at any time without changing client code. Central points can be private (require a Bearer token) or public (no auth required).
centralPoints.bullet1: Create central points in Settings → Central Points
centralPoints.bullet2: Assign an orchestrator to route traffic to it
centralPoints.bullet3: Swap the backing orchestrator at any time without client changes
centralPoints.bullet4: Private: requires Authorization Bearer header; Public: anonymous access
centralPoints.bullet5: Use the X-CENTRAL-ID and X-ORG-ID headers when calling the chat endpoint

faq1.question: Why use a Central Point instead of calling an orchestrator directly?
faq1.answer: Orchestrator IDs change when you recreate instances. A Central Point gives you a stable ID — swap the backing orchestrator any time without touching your client code or integrations.

faq2.question: What is the difference between private and public?
faq2.answer: Private central points require a Bearer token for every request. Public central points allow anonymous requests — useful for demos, embedded chat widgets, or open tools.

faq3.question: How do I call a Central Point from my app?
faq3.answer: POST to /api/chat/completion with headers X-ORG-ID and X-CENTRAL-ID. For private points, also send Authorization: Bearer {token}. The body is a standard messages array.

techSection.title: API & Access Reference

chat.desc: Streaming SSE endpoint. Same message format as direct orchestrator chat. Responds with the same SSE event types.

headers.title: Request Headers
headers.centralId: The stable ID of the central point. Required on all requests.
headers.orgId: The org this central point belongs to. Required on all requests.
headers.authorization: Bearer token. Required when visibility is private. Omit for public central points.

visibility.title: Visibility Modes
visibility.public: No authentication required. Any caller with the endpoint URL can send messages. Suitable for embeds and demos.
visibility.private: Requires Authorization: Bearer {token} from an org member. Suitable for internal tools and restricted access.

techSection.statusNote: Inactive central points return HTTP 404 to all callers regardless of visibility setting.
techSection.tierNote: Central points are a Pro+ tier feature. Creation is blocked when the org's max_central_points quota is reached.

useCasesSection.title: What You Can Do With Central Points
useCasesSection.intro: Central Points unlock patterns that would otherwise require changes to every app or integration that talks to your AI assistant. Here are the most common scenarios.

bluegreen.title: Safe Upgrades — No Downtime
bluegreen.scenario: You have a customer-facing chat widget that calls your assistant. You want to upgrade the assistant with new plugins and a new model — but you cannot risk breaking anything live. So you build and test the new version alongside the old one. When you're happy, you flip the Central Point to the new version. If something goes wrong, one more flip brings the old version back instantly.
bluegreen.benefit: Your customers never see an outage or a broken chat. The widget URL never changes.

rollback.title: Instant Rollback
rollback.scenario: You released a new orchestrator and users are reporting that answers are worse than before. You still have the previous version sitting idle. You reassign the Central Point back to it in seconds — no redeployment, no waiting for a build pipeline, no code change.
rollback.benefit: Rolling back an AI assistant is as fast as changing a setting.

testing.title: Test Before You Commit
testing.scenario: You want to try a new LLM model or an experimental plugin without exposing it to real users. Create a second private Central Point that points to the new orchestrator. Your QA team tests against the private point while production traffic keeps flowing through the stable one.
testing.benefit: Experiment freely without any risk to your live users.

public.title: Public Demo or Embedded Widget
public.scenario: You want to embed an AI assistant on a public webpage — no login required for visitors. Set the Central Point to public visibility. Anyone with the endpoint URL can chat with it. When you update the assistant, flip the Central Point; the widget never needs touching.
public.benefit: Ship a public chatbot without exposing internal orchestrator IDs or managing per-visitor tokens.

multienv.title: Separate Environments From One Codebase
multienv.scenario: Your app reads the Central Point ID from an environment variable. You create one Central Point per environment (dev, staging, production), each backed by a different orchestrator. The app code is identical across environments — only the environment variable changes.
multienv.benefit: Consistent code, environment-specific AI behaviour, zero hardcoded orchestrator IDs.

orgs.title: Organizations & Members
orgs.subtitle: Multi-tenant workspaces; users can belong to multiple orgs with different roles.
orgs.intro: Cadence is multi-tenant. Each organization is an isolated workspace with its own orchestrators, plugins, LLM configs, central points, and members. A user can be a member of multiple organizations simultaneously, switching between them via the org menu. Each org has an independent subscription tier and quota.
orgs.bullet1: Org admins add members by email, username, or user ID
orgs.bullet2: Promote members to org admin or demote back to member
orgs.bullet3: Remove members from the org without deleting their account
orgs.bullet4: Users switch orgs from the top-left org menu in the sidebar
orgs.bullet5: Subscription tier controls quotas: max orchestrators, members, messages, etc.

faq1.question: Can a user belong to multiple organizations?
faq1.answer: Yes. A user can be a member of multiple organizations simultaneously, with a different role in each one. Switch between them using the org menu at the top of the sidebar.

faq2.question: Who can create organizations?
faq2.answer: Only system admins can create organizations. Org admins manage the membership and settings of their own organization.

faq3.question: What happens if I am in an org but not an admin?
faq3.answer: You get the Member role, which grants access to Chat only. Orchestrators, plugins, settings, and admin tools are not visible.

techSection.title: Data Isolation & Quota Reference

isolation.title: Per-Org Isolated Resources
isolation.llmConfigs: API keys and provider configurations are scoped to this org only.
isolation.plugins: Uploaded plugin packages are not shared across orgs.
isolation.orchestrators: Orchestrator definitions and their pool instances belong to this org.
isolation.centralPoints: Central point IDs are unique within an org; no cross-org routing.
isolation.members: Membership and role assignments are per-org. A user may be a member of multiple orgs.

quotas.title: Subscription Quota Fields
quotas.maxOrchestrators: Maximum number of orchestrators allowed in this org. Enforced at creation time.
quotas.maxCentralPoints: Maximum number of central points. Requires Pro+ tier.
quotas.maxPlugins: Maximum number of uploaded plugin packages.
quotas.maxMembers: Maximum number of members in this org.

techSection.membershipNote: A user can belong to multiple orgs simultaneously. Role checks are always evaluated against the specific org of the request.
techSection.slugNote: The org slug is used in UI routing and cannot be changed after creation.

roles.title: Role-Based Access
roles.subtitle: Three roles: System Admin (platform), Org Admin (workspace), Member (chat only).
roles.intro: Access in Cadence is controlled by two orthogonal role dimensions: system-level (System Admin vs. regular user) and org-level (Org Admin vs. Member). A System Admin has full platform access regardless of org membership. An Org Admin controls everything within their organization. A Member can only use Chat.
roles.bullet1: System Admin: create orgs, manage users, Store (system plugins), global settings, pool, health
roles.bullet2: Org Admin: orchestrators, plugins, members, LLM configs, central points, settings
roles.bullet3: Member: Chat page only — cannot see settings, orchestrators, or plugins
roles.bullet4: Roles are enforced at both the API and UI layers
roles.bullet5: A user can be Org Admin in one org and Member in another simultaneously

faq1.question: What can a System Admin do?
faq1.answer: System admins have full platform access: create orgs and users, manage Store (system plugins), configure global settings, view pool stats, and check system health.

faq2.question: What can an Org Admin do?
faq2.answer: Org admins manage everything within their organization: orchestrators, plugins, members, LLM configs, central points, and org settings.

faq3.question: What can a Member do?
faq3.answer: Members can only access the Chat page. They cannot see orchestrators, plugins, or settings. They chat via loaded orchestrators or central points visible to their org.

techSection.title: Permission Reference
techSection.intro: Each role maps to a fixed permission set. Roles are assigned at the org membership level, not at the user level.

systemAdmin.perm2: Manage all users across all orgs; assign or revoke system_admin
systemAdmin.perm3: Read and write global settings (quotas, model catalog, feature flags)
systemAdmin.perm4: Purge org data and force-delete orchestrators

orgAdmin.perm1: Invite and remove org members; assign org_admin or member role
orgAdmin.perm2: Manage LLM configs, plugins, orchestrators, and central points
orgAdmin.perm3: Read org-level settings and quota usage
orgAdmin.perm4: Cannot access other orgs or platform-level settings

member.scope: Own org — read and chat only
member.perm1: Send messages to any active orchestrator in the org
member.perm2: Access public central points without auth; access private central points with Bearer token

techSection.crossOrgNote: A user can hold different roles in different orgs simultaneously — e.g., org_admin in Org A and member in Org B.
techSection.platformNote: system_admin is a platform-level attribute, not scoped to any single org.

admin.title: Admin Tools
admin.subtitle: Pool stats, health monitoring, tier quotas, and global settings — system admin only.
admin.intro: System admins have access to a dedicated System section in the sidebar with tools for platform operations: real-time pool statistics, service health checks, subscription tier quota management, a platform-wide LLM model catalog, and global feature settings that cascade to all organizations.
admin.bullet1: Pool: total instances, tier breakdown, shared models, memory estimate (auto-refresh 30s)
admin.bullet2: Health: PostgreSQL, Redis, MongoDB status + per-instance orchestrator readiness
admin.bullet3: Global Settings: subscription tier quotas, LLM model catalog, feature flags
admin.bullet4: Organizations: create, edit, set quotas, manage members across all orgs
admin.bullet5: Users: create, edit, delete platform users; grant or revoke system admin flag

faq1.question: What is the Pool page?
faq1.answer: The Pool page shows real-time statistics for all orchestrator instances across the platform: how many are hot/warm/cold, shared model count, and estimated memory usage. Auto-refreshes every 30 seconds.

faq2.question: What does the Health page show?
faq2.answer: The Health page shows the status of core services (PostgreSQL, Redis, MongoDB) and each loaded orchestrator instance with its ready state. Use it to diagnose outages or verify instances before routing traffic.

faq3.question: What are Global Settings?
faq3.answer: Global Settings are platform-wide defaults: subscription tier quotas, LLM provider model catalogs, and feature flags (pool behavior, caching, rate limiting, etc.). Some settings can be overridden per-organization by org admins.

globalSettings.title: Global Settings Fields
globalSettings.maxOrgs: Maximum number of organizations that can be created on this platform.
globalSettings.defaultTier: Default pool tier assigned to new orchestrators. One of hot, warm, cold.
globalSettings.hotQuota: Total number of Hot-tier pool slots available across the platform.
globalSettings.warmQuota: Total number of Warm-tier pool slots available across the platform.
globalSettings.coldQuota: Total number of Cold-tier pool slots available across the platform.
globalSettings.modelCatalog: Allowlist of model names that org admins are permitted to configure. Empty list means unrestricted.
globalSettings.featureFlags: Key-value flags for enabling experimental or gated features platform-wide.

health.title: Health Check Services
health.postgresql: Primary relational store. Reports status and query latency.
health.redis: Pool state and session cache. Reports status and ping latency.
health.mongodb: Conversation history and document store. Reports status and round-trip latency.

pool.title: Pool Tier Definitions
pool.hot: Fully loaded in memory. Zero cold-start latency. Highest memory cost.
pool.warm: Partially initialized. Sub-second warm-up on first request.
pool.cold: Config stored only. Full load on first request; highest latency.
pool.memoryNote: The pool stats panel shows real-time counts per tier and an estimated memory usage figure for Hot and Warm instances.

aiAppGraph.notLoaded: Orchestrator is not loaded. Use the Load button to initialize it, then refresh.
aiAppGraph.notAvailable: Graph visualization is not available for this orchestrator.

aiApps.title: AI Apps
aiApps.activateConfirm: Activate orchestrator "{name}"? This will enable the orchestrator.
aiApps.loadConfirm: Load orchestrator "{name}"? This will start the orchestrator.
aiApps.unloadConfirm: Unload orchestrator "{name}"? This will stop the orchestrator.

create.title: Create AI App
create.addPluginHint: Add at least one AI agent to configure.
create.whoamiPlaceholder: Optional context about who this AI App is (e.g. role, domain, tone). Injected into answer nodes.
create.nameMinLength: Name must be at least 10 characters
create.selectPluginRequired: Select at least one AI agent
create.llmConfigRequired: Default LLM Config is required for Supervisor mode
create.pluginsEmptyDesc: Add AI agents to extend your AI App's capabilities
create.pluginConfigurationDesc: Configure settings for each active AI agent.

grounded.predefinedContextDesc: Static context injected into every turn. Use for general instructions.
grounded.scopeRulesDesc: Define what is in and out of bounds. Leave empty to auto-generate from the anchor record.
grounded.scopeRulesPlaceholder: Leave empty to auto-generate from the record…
grounded.maxToolRoundsDesc: Maximum completed tool batches per user message (default: 3). When the cap is reached, pending tool calls are closed and the answer is synthesized. Validator replays do not count toward this limit.
grounded.enabledValidatorDesc: Add a validation step to check responses stay within the defined scope
grounded.singlePluginHint: Grounded mode requires exactly one scoped AI agent.
grounded.maxAgentHopsDesc: Maximum planner node visits per graph run (default: 25).

pluginSelector.title: Add AI agent
pluginSelector.noPluginsMatch: No AI agents match the filter.

edit.title: Edit Orchestrator
edit.nameMinLength: Name must be at least 10 characters
edit.nameMaxLength: Name must be at most 200 characters
edit.monitoringDescription: Send traces to an observability provider
edit.langfuseFieldsRequired: Secret Key, Public Key, and Host URL are required when monitoring is enabled

aiAppApi.failedUpdatePluginSettings: Failed to update AI agent settings
aiAppApi.failedActivatePluginVersion: Failed to activate AI agent version
